header.subtitle: Module 6: Skills — Tools that Claude uses for you
sec.conceito.intro: Skills extend what Claude can do. You create a SKILL.md file with instructions and Claude gains that tool. You can invoke it directly with /skill-name, but the powerful part is different: Claude can decide to use the skill on its own when it detects the skill is relevant to what you asked.
sec.conceito.card.invoke.title: You invoke
sec.conceito.card.invoke.desc: Type /deploy when you want to run the deploy. You control the timing — useful for workflows with side effects.
sec.conceito.card.auto.title: Claude invokes
sec.conceito.card.auto.desc: You ask "how does this code work?" and Claude detects that the explain-code skill is relevant and loads it automatically.
sec.conceito.legacy.title: There's another format: .claude/commands/
sec.conceito.legacy.desc: Before Skills, Claude Code used Commands — simple .md files in .claude/commands/. Both create slash commands and continue to work. Skills are the evolved format: they support YAML frontmatter, automatic invocation by Claude, support files, and subagent execution. See the full comparison in the section.
sec.conceito.where.title: Where Skills live
sec.conceito.where.priority: When skills share the same name at different levels, priority is: enterprise > personal > project.
sec.comparacao.intro: Both create slash commands invocable with /name. The difference is in how they are defined and what they can do. Skills are the recommended format — Commands continue to work and remain supported.
sec.comparacao.table.title: Feature comparison
sec.comparacao.table.row.slash: Creates /name command
sec.comparacao.table.row.disable: Invocation control (disable-model-invocation)
sec.comparacao.table.row.fork: Subagent execution (context: fork)
sec.comparacao.table.row.tools: Tool restriction (allowed-tools)
sec.comparacao.table.row.injection: Dynamic context injection (!`cmd`)
sec.comparacao.migrate.title: To migrate or not to migrate?
sec.comparacao.migrate.desc: Existing Commands don't need to be migrated. Keep using them if they meet your needs. Migrate to Skills when you want auto-invocation, support files, or control over when Claude can trigger them.
sec.comparacao.when.title: When to use each
sec.comparacao.when.li.1: Command: simple instruction, no invocation control needed
sec.comparacao.when.li.2: Skill: when you want Claude to know when to use it on its own, or you need frontmatter, support files, or an isolated subagent
sec.comparacao.precedence: Name conflict: if a Command and a Skill share the same name, the Skill takes precedence. Avoid duplicate names between .claude/commands/ and .claude/skills/.
sec.anatomia.intro: Each skill is a directory with a required SKILL.md file. The file has two parts: YAML frontmatter between --- that configures behavior, and the Markdown body with instructions. Click on each field to understand what it does.
sec.anatomia.structure.label: Directory structure
sec.anatomia.panel.default: Click on any frontmatter line to understand what it controls.
sec.anatomia.vars.title: Substitution Variables
sec.anatomia.vars.arguments.desc: Everything passed after the skill name.
sec.anatomia.vars.skilldir.desc: Skill directory. Useful for referencing bundled scripts.
sec.anatomia.vars.sessionid.desc: Current session ID. Useful for per-session logs.
sec.bundled.intro: Built-in skills ship with Claude Code and are available in any session. Unlike built-in commands that run fixed logic, these skills are prompt-driven — Claude orchestrates the work using its tools, can spawn parallel agents, and adapts execution to your project.
sec.bundled.batch.desc: Orchestrates large-scale codebase changes in parallel. Decomposes into 5–30 independent units and opens one PR per unit.
sec.bundled.batch.detail: Searches the codebase, presents the plan for approval, and only then spawns the agents. Requires a git repository.
sec.bundled.simplify.desc: Reviews recently changed files for reuse, quality, and efficiency issues — and fixes them.
sec.bundled.simplify.detail: Spawns 3 review agents in parallel, aggregates findings, and applies corrections. Can receive optional focus.
sec.bundled.loop.desc: Runs a prompt repeatedly at an interval while the session is open.
sec.bundled.loop.detail: Useful for monitoring deploys, babysitting PRs, or re-running another skill periodically.
sec.bundled.debug.desc: Enables debug logging for the current session and analyzes the log to diagnose issues.
sec.bundled.debug.detail: Debug is off by default. Running /debug mid-session starts capturing logs from that point on.
sec.bundled.api.desc: Loads Anthropic API and Agent SDK reference. Activates automatically when code imports anthropic or @anthropic-ai/sdk.
sec.bundled.api.detail: Classic auto-invocation example: you open a file that imports the Anthropic SDK and Claude already knows the API documentation without you having to ask.
sec.invocacao.intro: Two frontmatter fields control who can trigger a skill. Understanding them is essential for creating skills that behave as you expect.
sec.invocacao.matrix.title: Invocation Matrix
sec.invocacao.matrix.default.when: Reference or knowledge skills that Claude should apply when relevant.
sec.invocacao.matrix.disable.when: Workflows with side effects: /deploy, /commit, /send-slack. You control when to run.
sec.invocacao.matrix.userinv.when: Contextual knowledge that Claude uses internally. E.g.: legacy-system-context — doesn't make sense as a dev action.
sec.invocacao.example.manual.label: Manual skill: only you trigger it
sec.invocacao.example.manual.note: Claude never decides to deploy on its own even if the code looks ready.
sec.invocacao.example.context.label: Context skill: only Claude uses it
sec.invocacao.example.context.note: Claude loads this context when working on payments. /legacy-context doesn't appear in the menu.
sec.criando.intro: From zero to a working skill shared with your team in 4 steps.
sec.criando.step.1.title: Create the skill directory
sec.criando.step.1.desc: The directory name becomes the slash command. review-pr/ → /review-pr.
sec.criando.step.2.title: Write the SKILL.md with frontmatter
sec.criando.step.2.desc: The description is the most important field — it tells Claude when to use the skill automatically.
sec.criando.step.3.title: Test and refine
sec.criando.step.3.desc: Run it with a real PR from your team. Evaluate whether the output is precise and specific to your stack. Iterate 2–3 times on the description and instructions until the result is reliable.
sec.criando.step.4.title: Version and share
sec.criando.step.4.desc: Commit the .claude/skills/ directory. When cloning the repo, every dev already has the skill available. Treat skills as executable documentation — update them alongside the code.
sec.subskills.intro: A skill doesn't need to know everything at once. When the domain is large — a UI library, a design system, an API with dozens of endpoints — loading everything into context would be wasteful. The Index Pattern solves this: the SKILL.md becomes a lightweight map pointing to detailed files. Claude loads only what the task requires.
sec.subskills.without.title: Without the index pattern
sec.subskills.without.desc: All descriptions are loaded into context at session start. With 6 detailed skills, you consume hundreds of tokens before typing a single line.
sec.subskills.with.title: With the index pattern
sec.subskills.with.desc: Only the ui-library skill description enters the initial context. Detailed content for each component is only read when Claude needs it.
sec.subskills.flow.title: How it works at runtime
sec.subskills.flow.step.1: Session starts — only the ui-library description is loaded: "UI library guide. Use when working with Button, Modal, Table, DataGrid, Form, Tooltip."
sec.subskills.flow.step.2: You ask: "Create a DataGrid with multi-select and column sorting"
sec.subskills.flow.step.3: Claude invokes the skill and reads SKILL.md (index). Identifies the task is about DataGrid → reads only data-grid.md.
sec.subskills.flow.step.4: Executes the task with full knowledge about DataGrid — without having loaded Button, Modal, Form, or any other component.
sec.subskills.insight.title: The gain isn't just tokens — it's quality
sec.subskills.insight.desc: When Claude focuses only on the relevant sub-skill, it reads the document entirely and carefully — without splitting attention across 5 other components that have nothing to do with the task. The result is more precise, fewer hallucinated props, and conventions followed correctly.
sec.avancado.intro: Features that expand what skills can do beyond simple instructions.
sec.avancado.injection.intro: The !`command` syntax runs a shell command before the content is sent to Claude. The output replaces the placeholder — Claude receives real data, not the command itself.
sec.avancado.injection.how: How it works: Each !`command` runs immediately (pre-processing). The output is injected into the prompt. Claude receives the final result with real data — it doesn't see the commands themselves.
sec.avancado.fork.intro: context: fork runs the skill in an isolated subagent. The SKILL.md content becomes the prompt that drives the subagent, without access to the main conversation history.
sec.avancado.fork.card.1.title: 1. Isolated context
sec.avancado.fork.card.1.desc: Subagent has no access to the main session's history.
sec.avancado.fork.card.2.title: 2. Agent defines tools
sec.avancado.fork.card.3.title: 3. Result returns
sec.avancado.fork.card.3.desc: The subagent's output is summarized and returned to the main conversation.
sec.avancado.files.intro: The skill directory can have support files. The SKILL.md references them — Claude loads them only when needed, keeping the context lean.
sec.avancado.files.ref.label: Referencing in SKILL.md
sec.avancado.files.note: Detailed documentation, API specs, or example collections don't need to be loaded every time the skill runs. This avoids unnecessary context consumption.
sec.limites.intro: Misconfigured skills create unexpected behaviors — especially with automatic invocation.
sec.limites.anti.1.title: Vague description → Claude uses it when it shouldn't
sec.limites.anti.1.desc: Claude will interpret this as relevant for anything involving code — the skill fires in contexts you didn't anticipate.
sec.limites.fix.1.title: Description with specific triggers
sec.limites.anti.2.title: ❌ Deploy skill without disable-model-invocation
sec.limites.anti.2.desc: Claude may decide to run a deploy on its own when it detects the code "looks ready". Workflows with irreversible side effects always need disable-model-invocation: true.
sec.limites.fix.2.title: Separate knowledge skills from action skills
sec.limites.fix.2.desc: Skills that inform (conventions, legacy context) → let Claude invoke. Skills that act (deploy, commit, send message) → disable-model-invocation: true.
sec.limites.anti.3.title: ❌ Skill with context: fork without a clear task
sec.limites.anti.3.desc: A context: fork on a style guide skill ("use these conventions...") makes the subagent receive guidelines but no actionable task — it returns without doing anything.
sec.limites.fix.3.title: ✅ context: fork only for tasks with explicit instructions
sec.limites.fix.3.desc: Use fork for skills that have a clear task: research, report generation, script execution. For reference knowledge, use the default mode (inline).
sec.limites.budget.title: Skills context budget
sec.limites.budget.desc: Skill descriptions are loaded into context so Claude knows what's available. With many skills, they can exceed the budget (2% of the context window, max ~16,000 chars). Run /context to check if any skills are being excluded.
sec.proximos.intro: One simple skill this week. A team catalog next month.
sec.proximos.step.1.title: Explore the built-in skills already available
sec.proximos.step.1.desc: Before creating anything, try /simplify on your current project. See how the skill pattern works in practice.
sec.proximos.step.2.title: Create your first knowledge skill
sec.proximos.step.2.desc: Start with a context skill without side effects. E.g.: your project conventions that Claude should always follow when editing code.
sec.proximos.step.3.title: Create an action skill with disable-model-invocation: true
sec.proximos.step.3.desc: Pick a task you repeat manually: PR review, commit message, deploy checklist. Set disable-model-invocation: true to keep full control.
sec.proximos.step.4.title: Build the catalog with your team
sec.proximos.step.4.desc: Run a 30-min session: "What repetitive task do you want to delegate?" Every answer is a potential skill. Distribute creation, commit .claude/skills/, and share via git.
sec.proximos.mantra.label: Part 6 Mantra:
sec.proximos.mantra.text: "A well-described skill isn't a shortcut — it's Claude learning when and how to help, without you having to ask."
